# **Overview** {#overview}

Lennar’s cloud—or online—environment connects with our on-premise infrastructure using **Microsoft’s Azure Cloud Services (Azure).** Azure offers our teams a single sign-on setup that can manage access to services and resources such as storage accounts, databases, analytics, software, and networking services that support our IT standards. These services are grouped and privatized together based on Lennar’s purchased subscription model in Azure’s cloud service structure.

* **Software as a Service (SaaS) –** SaaS delivers services from Azure to our users by mirroring our infrastructure, middleware, app software, and app data in the cloud. In doing so, Azure is able to manage Lennar’s hardware and software, and—with the appropriate service agreement—will ensure the availability and the security of our data as well.

* **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** – IaaS allows our system engineers to use Azure’s virtualized computing resources to create, store, and maintain data over the internet so that our information and resources are secure, available, and backed-up in the event of a system failure.

* **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** – PaaS provides a platform for our system engineers to develop, run, and manage applications without the complexity involved in maintaining a physical infrastructure.

![][image1]

All of Lennar’s cloud-computing-related activities can be categorized into three main groups:  **Services**, **Management**, and **Provisioning**.

* **Services** are IT resources, such as deployment capabilities, monitoring alerts, and other various cloud-based utilities made available because of our online access.  
* **Management** on the cloud allows our administrators to activate, remove, or suspend accounts, licenses, or access across the entire company.  
* **Provisioning** allows administrators to authorize users to set up and launch applications in our cloud-computing environment.

These activities are backed-up and monitored to ensure our environment is protected from failures or shutdowns during maintenance periods. Custom backups or monitoring can be configured upon request.

To ensure administrators can manage these activities, all of our user accounts are configured using Lennar’s **Azure** **Active Directory (Azure AD)** server and automatically sync with our Microsoft Office365 environment using **Microsoft’s Azure Connect Server**. In this way, our administrators control access to our system and support our departments with account validation and reporting. The technical standards required to maintain our Azure environment are detailed with step-by-step instructions in the sections herein.

# **Technical Standards** {#technical-standards}

**Azure** is a comprehensive set of cloud services that developers and IT professionals use when building, deploying, and managing applications through Microsoft’s global network of datacenters. Lennar integrates Azure’s tools, DevOps, and marketplace to efficiently build anything that benefits our clients and business model, from simple mobile apps to internet-scale solutions.

## **Naming Convention** {#naming-convention}

Lennar’s naming rules and restrictions ensure our Azure resources are structured for two specific purposes: **Location** and **Limitations**.

* **Locations** for resources can be a server, database, subscription, or folder.  
* **Limitations** refer to any authorization or authentication steps required to access certain resources.

Names must meet the requirements of their specific resource type. When naming Azure subscriptions, verbose names make understanding the context and purpose of each subscription clear. When working in an environment with many subscriptions, following a shared naming convention can improve clarity.

|  Naming Convention Rationale |
| ----- |
| Lennar’s naming convention ensures our teams can accurately search and identify resources for development, provisioning, management, and cost analysis. |
| A resource’s environment and function should be listed in its name to accurately describe its application, i.e., DB, SQL, or WEB, and location. |
| Dashes are used to make names easily recognizable by splitting the name components out based on location and function. |

Approved prefixes or suffixes used to identify the type and context of the resource are used in our naming convention to be sure our resources properly identify their services. While all the information about type, metadata, context, is available in the file path, applying these approved affixes in the name simplifies this information visually for easy identification.

Consistent naming conventions make resources easier to locate. They can also indicate the role of a resource in a solution. The key to success with naming conventions is establishing and following them across your applications and organizations. Cloud Naming Standards should be used for the creation of any new Azure service or server. Lennar applies these rules when naming servers and services.

* Only use approved names.  
* Only lowercase text is allowed.  
* Every field should adhere to a fixed predetermined length.  
* 15-character limit to comply with legacy limitations.

![][image2]

Certain resources restrict the use of a separator and will instead be shown as one word. Otherwise, the naming convention ensures the important information required to manage our infrastructure remains clear and organized.

## **Subscriptions** {#subscriptions}

Lennar’s enterprise agreement with **Microsoft’s Azure Cloud** services enrolls our users into a multitude of Azure resources. Access to each resource is granted by adding users to a **Subscription**, which groups users with the resources that they’ll need for their projects. These subscriptions are further organized into a **Tenant** by **Azure AD**. Here, subscription services can be activated for all users belonging to the tenant to better manage groups of users and track our cost, as well as secure a dedicated instance of Azure AD for each of Lennar’s subsidiaries.

|  Subscription Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Each enrollment is billed separately by Microsoft, yet they still benefit from group pricing because our enterprise agreement has 5 enrollments. |
| Our enrollment structure for production, test development, sandbox, and active direction services enables our teams to benefit from our total infrastructure while operating as separate services to reduced costs and manage usage. |

Once a tenant is created, a tenant ID is generated for the tenant and saved in a highly secure internal Azure AD database. A privileged account owner can then log in to an Azure portal and add users to the newly created **Azure AD Tenant**. Lennar and its subsidiaries are separated into 3 enrollments: **Lennar**, **CalAtlantic Title**, and **EHM**.

![][image3]

Because of regulation reasons, Eagle Home Mortgage acts has its own enterprise agreement and act as a completely separate company from Lennar. Yet, we include them in our enrollment structure because they help qualify our entire organization for Microsoft’s group pricing.

The subscriptions under our enrollments include services inside **Sandbox**, **DevTest**, and **Production** environments. Our Azure AD environment manages which users have access to certain services inside these environments to ensure our teams can successfully meet our technical standards deadlines and benchmarks.

## **Resource Group** {#resource-group}

At Lennar, our Azure Cloud resources are assigned to **Resource Groups** using Azure AD. Resource groups are accessed through assigned privileges and roles in the directory or external applications or services. This includes all the resources involved in a solution or only those resources that users would need to complete their work. 

|  Resource Group Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Lennar’s Azure Cloud resources have to be logically contained so administrators can more easily support and monitor their use. |
| Organizing Azure Cloud resources into resource groups grants teams a single location for their work that administrators can manage. |
| The best logical grouping of our azure services combines application with environment. This allows us to easily setup up appropriate tags for pricing purposes. Will discuss this in the rationale. |

For example, a resource group containing an SQL Database, Web App, Storage Account, and/or Virtual Machine grants a team-controlled access to prevent unauthorized use of resources under their responsibility. Naming resource groups requires an understanding of their function and location. For instance, a resource group has a **one-to-many** relationship between **tenant ID** and **subscription ID**. This restricts resource groups to a specific application and environment because each subscription can only be assigned to one tenant.

The resource group name uses the resource itself and joins it with the environment it belongs to in our system. 

Example \= Resource Group \= \<app\>\<environment\>;

     wkdydev; wkdyprod; wkdysnd; wkdyad

| Resource Group Naming Convention |  | ENVIRONMENT |  |  |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
|  |  | **Sandbox** | **Development** | **Production** |
| **APPLICATION** | **WorkDay** |  **WKDY SBX **  |  **WKDY DEV **  |  **WKDY PRD **  |
|  | **Chatbot** |  **CHTBT SBX **  |  **CHTBT DEV **  |  **CHTBT PRD **  |
|  | **Business Intelligence** |  **BI SBX** |  **BI DEV **  |  **BI PRD **  |

**Table 1: Resource Group Naming Table**

## **Azure Networking** {#azure-networking}

Lennar’s **Azure Virtual Network (VNet)** is a representation of our on-premise network in the cloud. Microsoft automatically updates our VNet to match our on-premise network based on the connection settings controlled by our network operations engineers. One important VNet connection we use is called **ExpressRoute**, which links our on-premise and cloud network into a hybrid solution for provisioning and managing our Azure services.

|  Azure Networking Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| AT\&T has been a Lennar business partner for many years and our history provides us discounts and familiarity with their private peer process. |
| Organizing our data center into a private network that connects with our internet provider (AT\&T) and then to Microsoft using private peering increases the amount of network coverage we have while improving our latency performance. |

Our data centers connect to our VNet through connects like ExpressRoute, which then connects to the cloud through our network provider, AT\&T. From here, AT\&T connects our VNet to Microsoft using private peering to sync our on-premise network with Azure for its cloud services. Specific change points are configured in our network for AT\&T to control what parts of our environment we want to connect to Azure and then our subscription groups use this configuration to deliver our services to users.  
![][image4]

The network traffic flows between the on-premises network and an Azure VNet through an IPSec VPN tunnel (VNet gateway), which is a secure network protocol suite of IPv4 managed by our network engineers that interfaces with **Azure’s Network Security Group (NSG)**. Creating virtual network gateway can take up to 45 minutes to complete. You can find more information about this process at [https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-overview](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-overview).

![][image5]

Lennar circuit users connect 80% of Lennar’s on-premise networking environment through ExpressRoute for more reliability, security, and better performance. If necessary, this connection can be revoked. This is accomplished by an authorization key in Azure that can be used by a circuit user to change the connection’s status.

## **Backup and Restore** {#backup-and-restore}

Lennar uses Azure’s built-in data management tool called **Commvault** for backups and restores. Commvault is built into Azure as a single sign-on platform, which enables our security team to enforce centralized policies that assure our network’s overall availability, usability and as it’s used within our enterprise agreement with Microsoft.

|  Backup and Restore Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Commvault’s Azure online and on-demand storage capabilities ensure our data needs can be met by scaling our use in the cloud with better costs and full backup support. |
| Commvault centralizes our data through its single sign-on portal, which administrators can use to better manage our data in the event of a local disaster requiring a remote system recovery. |

Lennar’s data network engineers have configured Commvault to backup our company’s data on Azure every night, starting at 11:30 p.m. If a separate backup is required, then a request must be submitted to the backup administrators through **ServiceNow (SNow)**. SNow requests for backups or restores must include the specific parameters required for the backup or restore so that the backup administrators can begin to schedule the process. 

PaaS resources are backed up and restored during disaster recovery (DR) events using Azure’s built in **Operations Management Suite (OMS)**. This infrastructure supports several disaster recovery scenarios, such as system-provided geo-replication of Azure Storage to secondary regions. Configured backups will copy our on-premise environment on Azure with some minor DNS changes so that apps using public DNS addresses can use the Azure published address. This will protect our data during our failover procedure.

![][image6]

The estimated time of backup or recovery depends on several factors including the total number of databases recovering in the same region at the same time, the database size, the transaction log size, and network bandwidth. Typically, it takes less than 12 hours though it may take longer to backup or recover a very large or active database.

### **Azure SQL Database Backup and Restore** {#azure-sql-database-backup-and-restore}

Our SQL databases use Commvault to create full, differential, and transaction log backups for the purposes of a **point-in-time restore (PITR)**. Performing backups in the **SQL Server Azure** environment starts when a backup administrator receives an approved backup request ticket submitted to the system engineering and operations team via SNow.

The transaction log backups generally happen every 5 to 10 minutes, with the frequency based on the performance level and amount of database activity occurring at that time. When you restore a database, the service automatically figures out which full, differential, and transaction log backups need to be restored.

#### Dev, UAT, or ProD Environment Backup and Restore

Backing up Lennar’s development (DEV), user acceptance testing (UAT), or production (PROD) environments requires a database administrator to use Azure’s portal page when a ticket request comes in from ServiceNow. To start, a subscription needs to be selected and a resource group needs to be configured to follow a specific backup or restore procedure, just set the parameters for which one is needed.

![][image7]

### **PaaS Backup and Restore** {#paas-backup-and-restore}

Lennar’s PaaS environment is managed in **Azure’s Operations Management Suite (OMS)**. Like IaaS, PaaS includes infrastructure—servers, storage, and networking—but also middleware, development tools, business intelligence (BI) services, database management systems, and more are backed up every night at 11:30 p.m.

|  PaaS Backup and Restore Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Using Azure’s OMS capabilities provides a cost-saving incentive for Lennar given its built-in backup and restoration services that are supported by Microsoft, free-of-charge. |
| Centralizing our backup and restoration process in Azure allows our system administrators to manage all of our database and virtual machines with increased performance and flexibility due to only requiring one application to handle the process. |

These backups and restorations are configured according to ticket requests in **SNow**. Administrators complete backups by logging into portal.azure.com and accessing the appropriate environment and configure the necessary parameters outlined in the ticket requests.

![][image8]

Azure allows accounts to be configured how they need to be for the owner to be satisfied with their backup process. This includes configuring the amount that needs to be stored, where it needs to be stored, and how often a backup needs to occur.  Lennar’s web hosting environment is backed up every day at 11:30 p.m. All of our on-premise data center information is replicated and backed-up by Azure.

#### Web App Backups

Scheduling a custom backup for a web app begins when a SNow ticket is submitted and received by the DSS team. A member of the team will then setup the backup and ensure it is performed properly by viewing the log information in Microsoft Azure.

System engineers will open the resource group identified in the SNow ticket found in Commvault. Afterward, they will follow the configuration specifications from the SNow ticket to setup the requested backup. When the correct configuration is in place, that backup will be scheduled and show up in the log report.

![][image9]

#### Storage Accounts

OMS helps contain Lennar’s on-premise resources and data objects in personalized storage accounts to be managed on the cloud. Each account is labeled with a unique namespace and access is given to members that are assigned to contribute to the account. All objects in a storage account are billed together as a group.

![][image10]

If the needs of a backup exceed the scalability targets of a single storage account, multiple storage accounts with multiple disks can be assigned for extra disk space.  Azure’s pricing calculator can be used to determine the cost of any addition storage space that exceed the subscription amount determined in Lennar’s enterprise agreement.

### **IaaS Backup and Restore**	 {#iaas-backup-and-restore}

Lennar’s Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) environment uses **Commvault** to backup, maintain, and protect our cloud infrastructure. Commvault allows Microsoft to support our infrastructure by automatically detecting our on-premise virtual machines and connects them to Azure. In this way, our database administrators are able to set up thresholds to monitor and log our backup process infrastructure. All of our IaaS information is accessible through Azure’s central dashboard for review and configuration, upon request.

|  IaaS Backup and Restore Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Commvault uses a single dashboard for all our backups that allows our team to monitor our infrastructure activity using performance and capacity threshold reports. |
| Using a single-sign on service like Azure makes it easy to track all of our applications and environments across our enrollment structure. |

Any new servers created in our environment will automatically follow our backup schedule. If a failure occurs, Commvault sends automated emails to our development service solutions (DSS) team, and they will investigate why the failure occurred and solve it within 3 business days. Once a solution is discovered, the DSS team will revert the failed system to a recovery point and restore it using the Commvault console.  
![][image11]

Commvault protects and secures our critical company data residing on laptops and desktops with an automated backup service that doesn’t interrupt user productivity. It also minimizes data leakage with built-in security settings that allow administrators to encrypt files and folders and securely wipe data from lost or stolen laptops. This increases our security teams’ visibility and control over endpoint data for compliance and litigation purposes with integrated full-text search and reporting from a consolidated pool of endpoint data.

## **Monitoring and Alerts** {#monitoring-and-alerts}

Our system engineers configure log alerts to leverage the **Azure Log Analytics** service to managed resources across different subscriptions on a centralized log. Custom queries are then used based on the logs to make insights into applications and their status. This helps ensure that monitoring and alerts can be configured for any cloud service that gets put into the production environment.

|  Monitoring and Alert Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Azure alerts report the status of our enterprise agreement automatically and can be set up to include new alerts using the single sign-on dashboard in a fast and efficient manner. |
| OMS can be used to monitor our entire company’s subscription model and tracks each resource group to provide the highest possible version control across our company’s cloud and on-premise environment |

PaaS, IaaS, and storage monitoring and alerts on Azure are automatically set up on the cloud when deployed on-premise and are further managed through **Azure’s Operations Management Suite (OMS).** This service platform allows administrators access to all of our on-premise and cloud IT assets from one console. Furthermore, administrators are able to control deployments, operations, and decommissioning of workloads and resources while monitoring the overview of what data is being logged and checked into our environment.

### **Azure SQL Database** {#azure-sql-database}

SQL Database uses SQL Server technology to create backups for the purposes of Point-in-time restore (PITR).  the frequency based on the performance level and amount of database activity. Transaction log backups, with full and differential backups, allow you to restore a database to a specific point-in-time to the same server that hosts the database. When you restore a database, the service figures out which full, differential, and transaction log backups need to be restored. Monitoring is done at the database level. The monitoring we establish in this environment will be used in all Azure Workday environments. The environments are **DEV**, **QA**, **UAT**, and **PROD**. Data warehouse monitoring and alerts can also be configured in Microsoft Azure. Custom alerts can be requested through SNow

**Azure SQL Database**

* **Resource** – sqlservername/databasename (servers/database)


  * **Name** – sitenameDBAlert


    * **Metric** – Workers percentage


    * **Condition** – set to greater than


    * **Threshold** – 70%


    * **Period** – Over the last 5 minutes. 

  ![][image12] 

#### DTU Percentage

![][image13]

#### CPU Usage Over 90 Percent

If the processor cycles are running at 90% for longer than five minutes, we should investigate. In practice, we may extend this to 15 minutes. 

It is possible that highly-computational multi-threaded transactions can put us at this threshold for longer than five minutes. Proper configuration is performed in **sys.configurations** tables, where ‘**max degree of parallelism**’ and ‘**cost threshold for parallelism**’ values are set. 90 percent threshold in a highly computational multi-threaded environment may not be out of the ordinary.

If there are multiple cores and only few are operating at that level, more cores (threads) will eventually be added to the transaction and remaining threads operating at lower cycles can handle other processes normally.

![][image14]

#### 

#### Log IO Over 85 Percent

This may be revised upward over 85 percent following testing. Presently, we are at 85 percent over 10 minutes. This may be extended as a result of testing. We do not know for certain the types of load operations we will experience until the conclusion of actual load

![][image15]

#### Data IO Over 85 percent

High data loading is typical during peak operations. If we see this behavior for an extended period, however, we should issue an alert. Presently, we are at 85 percent over 10 minutes. This may be extended as a result of testing. We do not know for certain the types of load operations we will experience until the conclusion of actual load testing.

![][image16]

#### Failed Connections

This alert notifies us of failed connections. We will have to test this under actual conditions to verify threshold and interval. We are presently at three instances over a five minutes period. Operationally, databases sometimes experience failed connection attempts. Failures range from expired users to incorrect passwords. The objective is to receive an alert where there is a pattern of behavior. 

![][image17]

#### Deadlock Notification

We are looking for any deadlocks over a 5-minute interval. Deadlocks differ from blocking, which represents a temporary condition. Deadlocks result in a stoppage until one of the deadlocking transactions is rolled back. Roll back us usually performed by SQL Server.

#### Database Storage 90 Percent Full

![][image18]

When the allocated storage space reaches 90% full, an alert is sent. At this point, additional storage space can be purchased, but Lennar managers make the decision decide if the cost is worth it or if archiving, deleting, or increasing storage allocation is the best option.

### **PaaS Monitoring and Alerts** {#paas-monitoring-and-alerts}

Lennar’s platform as a service (PaaS) is a complete development and deployment environment in the cloud, with resources that enable our system engineers to deliver our Azure subscription services to our users. This includes simple cloud-based apps to sophisticated, cloud-enabled enterprise applications. All of Lennar’s PaaS components are accessed through subscriptions billed as a part of its enterprise agreement with Microsoft Azure.

Regarding the alerts, Lennar monitors HTTP errors on web applications and CPU and memory on the application service level agreements. PaaS makes it possible to change or adapt these environments based on new best practices and improvements.

|  PaaS Monitoring and Alert Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Azure alerts automatically track and report the status every production component and can be set up to include new alerts using the single sign-on dashboard, saving money on integration costs and avoiding incompatibilities with other software. |
| PaaS uses Azure alerts because it is an expansive and comprehensive COTS solution that integrates well with our network and cloud services. |

Each PaaS production component requires monitoring to ensure Microsoft’s service level agreement (SLA) with Lennar is met. This functionality is built into Azure in the Overview section. Administrators with access to the resource group service can monitor and review the alerts for each resource group monitored in the cloud. In the event of an event that triggers a PaaS production component alert, an email will be sent to the NOC and DSS administrator to correct.

![][image19]

#### WebHosting

Monitoring and alerts for web applications are configured using the **Azure portal webpage**. Alerts for data caps, storage limits, or access can be configured for all applications and databases after logging in as a privileged user. At Lennar, our database administrators are monitoring HTTP errors on our Web applications, as well as CPU and memory statuses on the applications service plans.

External tools at [UptimeRobot.com](http://UptimeRobot.com) and [PingDom.com](http://PingDom.com) are used to monitor our website’s uptime by reporting on performance metrics related to our internet connection and application response times.

![][image20]

#### Angular Web APP

#### Java Web App

#### Node Web App

### **IaaS Monitoring and Alerts** {#iaas-monitoring-and-alerts}

Lennar’s Infrastructure as a service (IaaS) is a form of cloud computing that provides virtualized computing resources from our Azure subscriptions over the internet. Our database engineers monitor our resource groups using Azure’s **Operations Management Suite** (OMS). OMS is a collection of management services that are hosted entirely in Azure, certifying all components and data for OMS are stored in Azure for resource adjustments, as needed. To access OPM, database engineers an internet connection and browser with the OMS log in analysis addon for Azure. Once logged in, resources can be reviewed and updated based on what resource groups are being logged and checked. 

|  IaaS Monitoring and Alert Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| OMS offers continuous access to the latest information about our cloud data and is maintained by Microsoft to use the best security and data management practices in the industry without any additional cost. |
| Access to OPM only requires an internet connection and browser with the OMS log in analysis addon for Azure. Once logged in, our infrastructure team can review and update what resource groups are being logged and checked across all of our subscription services. |

Requests for data logging and monitoring begins with a request from **SNow** so that our database administrators know which resource groups to configure. Once the ticket for monitoring is requested and approved, a runbook in Azure Automation drives a failover process with **Azure Site Recovery** and then logs the information to Azure’s **Log Analytics** to generate an alert.

Configuring our resource groups costs money, requiring a checks-and-balance setup before an alert for a resource group can be created. Examples of alerts are databases that report email count threshold alerts or performance alerts.

![][image21]

Azure alerts are set up to resolve themselves within 5-seconds by allocated the required bandwidth. Any alerts that are not automatically addressed will have to be handled by a database engineer.

### **Storage Accounts Monitoring and Alerts** {#storage-accounts-monitoring-and-alerts}

Typically, our users send ticket requests for alerts and thresholds for data objects. The volume of data objects within a resource group are kept on our Backup Server v2 database. When an increase in the volume of data objects for a resource group is requested, the server is formatted to use the Resilient File System (ReFS), which Microsoft’s modern backup storage process requires.

|  Storage Monitoring and Alert Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Azure allows our database engineers to ensure superior data integrity by monitoring a changed data object across all of our subscriptions, ensuring our data logs are always updated with the latest information |
| Storage in Azure automatically configures geo-replication options in a single menu, empowering our backup process with enhanced global and local access and business continuity. |

The total uptime, errors, CPU usage, response time, and volume are tracked for a variety of storage. Data objects that receive alerts may be for the following:

* Angular Web Apps  
* Node Web Apps  
* Java Web Apps  
* Blob Storage Accounts  
* SQL Objects (servers/databases)

Each data object can be configured as needed by a team, especially when more data objects are required for projects or if a project needs to be closed out and authorization must be restricted. You can configure monitoring via the **Azure** portal or **Windows PowerShell**, or programmatically via one of the storage client libraries.

Alerts in the Azure portal can automatically notify our data administrators of important changes in the behavior of our storage services. If you use a storage explorer tool to download this metrics data in a delimited format, you can use Microsoft Excel to analyze the data. See **Azure Storage Client Tools** for a list of available storage explorer tools. You can configure alerts in the Alert rules pane, accessible under Monitoring in the Storage account menu pane.

For a selected subscription, logs can be read and analyzed:

* Triggered alerts and alert sources \- This table shows summary counts, alert sources, and how many times alerts fired for the selected time duration. It applies to both older and newer alerts. Read more about the newer Azure Alerts.  
* Activity Log Errors \- If any of your Azure resources log events with error-level severity, you can view a high-level count and click through to the activity log page to investigate each event.

Retention periods can be set up to decide how long the storage service keeps the metrics and charges Lennar for the space required to store the analyzed data. A retention period should be chosen so that there is sufficient time to analyze the data and download any metrics you wish to keep for off-line analysis or reporting purposes. Lennar is also billed for downloading metrics data from storage accounts. After a storage account’s analytics are configured, the gathered information can be viewed in the Azure portal in that show hourly metrics of monitored storage accounts. 

#### Blob Storage Accounts Monitoring and Alerts

## **Azure Cloud Security** {#azure-cloud-security}

Lennar’s Azure security protocol calls for a review of all outputs from each service packaged in our subscriptions. These outputs must then be divided into relative security domains to prioritize a path to remediate the any security risks found in the data. Additionally, the general methodology of utilizing Role-Based Access Controls through an auditable and frequently reviewed process should be adopted. This follows CSA v3.0 (NIST 800-53R) and other standards that define user access control controls.  

|  Security Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Role-based permission security standards control access to resources to ensure resources are only able to be used by those that need them. |
| Isolating resources from the web using Application Service Environments (ASEs) allows our security team to monitor authorization and authentication policies with fewer security vulnerabilities |

### **User Access Controls** {#user-access-controls}

Subscriptions are managed through role permissions in Azure. Administrator roles, or owners, should be limited to 2 or 3 associates, each with segregated duties. These roles are assigned per resource group or subscription and inherit their permissions for any child objects within. Contributor roles are used for resource group and resource assignment; these are target assignments and not inherited from subscription-level membership. Chiefly, permissions for subscriptions should be granted for the least number of users, with the least privilege, and for the least amount of time.  

### **Encryption** {#encryption}

Currently, Lennar Enterprise Security Office Policy requires encryption of data at rest.  When considering encryption in cloud environments, policies from NIST, SANS20, CIS, and CSA v3.0 recommend that encryption for data in transit should also be required.  Specific controls should impose that intra-cloud communication and external integrations should only occur via HTTPS or other secure methodology.

Storage accounts, VM, IaaS service, and servers are encrypted on the OS (internal drive) and in BHD (attached drives to this IAAS).

1. All data storage at rest—storage accounts (blob), virtual machine (OS, attached disk), SQL databases, and all other relevant solutions— must utilize Azure Storage Service Encryption (AES-256) or another equivalent configuration. 

1. Azure Managed Storage Service Encryption (SSE) is adequate.

2. Using Azure Key Vault for internally managed key services is strongly preferred and should be included in service maturity plan.  With Key Vault, the responsibility for the control and manageability of encryption services would shift to Lennar IT/ESO.

3. Where applicable (storage accounts with internet-facing accessibility, Application Services), ‘HTTPS only’ must be selected as the method by which those systems communicate.

### **Network Security** {#network-security}

Depending on the types of systems and their configuration in a cloud deployment, Network Security controls are focused on the security boundaries in an environment.  Security boundaries are typically identified as the location where two tiers/zones meet, each tier/zone having different security labels.  This is most easily demonstrated in the example of an internet-facing website; there is a security boundary where the website meets the internet.  Security boundaries must be identified and security solutions (web application firewall, next-generation firewall) must be implemented to secure systems and inspect network traffic that transgresses the boundaries.

Microsoft Application Gateway is used as our load balancer. It is a layer 7 device that looks at traffic and performs encryption, certificates, determine application data specifics.

1. Web Applications and Web Services that are public facing must be behind a web application firewall or equivalent.    
   1) The Microsoft Application Gateway, configured in WAF mode, satisfies this requirement.  
      2) Where necessary (a requirement for advanced features or significantly larger resources) and when a MS App Gateway WAF is inadequate, it is expected to adopt and utilize an advanced 3rd\-party solution such as F5, Barracuda, or Positive Application FW)  
         2. For other internet-facing solutions such as storage accounts, SQL PaaS solutions, and exposed API or REST services, it is necessary to use the native ‘Firewall’ configuration within each solution to apply IP restrictions for access or utilize a virtual network and Network Security Group to secure traffic via IP and Port restrictions.  
1) As part of a service maturity roadmap, API and other external integrations should leverage application services such as an ‘API Management Gateway’ instead of depending on traditional localized, independently configured network security controls.  
   3. The security boundary between Azure and the Lennar internal network must also be considered, if a particular subscription requires communication with Lennar On-Premise networks or systems.  If necessary, a network virtual appliance or virtual Palo Alto firewall is required to secure and inspect the traffic destined for the internal network.  
1) A Microsoft Network Virtual Appliance is adequate for Subscription-to-Subscription and intra-VNET segmentation.    
2) In order to match or exceed the protection between similar boundaries as the Cloud (Azure) – Internal Network, a next-generation firewall technology, such as a virtual Palo Alto firewall is required.  Specifically, the solution must provide Application Layer visibility/Layer-7, IDS/IPS, and integrated threat intelligence.  
   4. Web Applications or App Services must utilize SSL Certificates to achieve a forced connection or redirection to HTTPS/443. 

### **Endpoint Protection** {#endpoint-protection}

1. Current Enterprise Security Office policy requires that all endpoints and servers have endpoint protection installed.  This policy extends to virtual and cloud environments.    
2. Deploy in VM, you use extensions for logging, activating utility. Cylance is ran with MSEP. It effective COTS. Review signature endpoint protection. (at the same time, we have 4 pieces of security and they all have to be setup to work together. Get a list of them from Josh. Talk about Cylance, but then a bullet and diagram showing our software stack. This stack must be deployed on every VM or IAAS.

1. All applicable systems should automatically be configured for the MS Anti-Malware Protection solution in Azure.  It is strongly preferred that this extension is automatically deployed to VM’s and other applicable systems as they are initially deployed.

2. A solution maturity roadmap must include the deployment of ‘Cylance’ as an additional, advanced endpoint protection agent. Utilizing Desired State Configuration (DSC), the Cylance agent must be configured to programmatically be installed upon deployment of applicable resources.

### **Security Monitoring**  {#security-monitoring}

1. SANS, NIST, and the CSA v3.0 require a centralized log management system or Security Incident and Event Management solution for more advanced security monitoring.  Azure Security Center should be utilized as the ‘cloud-SIEM’, with a plan to integrate these logs into the Enterprise SIEM in the near-term.  The integration of the Cloud-SIEM data with the Enterprise SIEM (Splunk) is imperative to ensure that Enterprise-wide correlation and advanced analytics can occur over all security events.

1. All applicable systems must have the Azure Security Center or Monitoring agent installed programmatically upon deployment.  Additionally, the Azure Security Center should be the collector or SIEM in the cloud; therefore, other relevant diagnostic logging, alerts, and application activity should be configured to forward to Security Center.

2. Splunk is the Enterprise SIEM utilized by the ESO/Security Operations Center.  Where applicable and if possible, logs collected in the Security Center will be forwarded to Splunk for ingestion.  A path for full integration between the two log aggregating systems must be developed and implemented.

3. Azure and Subscription-level security relevant logging will need to be configured to maximize monitoring and visibility.  Azure Activity Logs, Azure Active Directory Audit Logs, and Security Policy Violation logs must be configured for aggregation in the Security Center or direct integration with Splunk via the Log Integration connector.

4. Alerts, such as SQL Audit and Threat Detection, that can be configured with a notification to an email account, must be configured with ‘SOC@Lennar.com’ as the recipient.

### **Threat & Vulnerability Management** {#threat-&-vulnerability-management}

1. As Lennar On-Premise policy requires and most standards specify, it is necessary to implement vulnerability scanning and assessment in the cloud.

1. On applicable systems, it is required that a vulnerability assessment solution is configured.  It is preferred that this programmatically enabled as part of the initial deployment of that service.

2. Initially, the Microsoft Vulnerability Assessment, available in many PaaS solutions such as SQL dB, is an adequate solution for this requirement.

1. As part of a service maturity roadmap, all applicable systems will be required to have continuous scanning/assessment via Rapid7 (the ‘On-Premise’ TVM standard) or a comparable system.

2. Systems Updates should be managed, and the process governed as it is On-Premise.  The Security Center or Monitoring Agent/Extension will report on necessary updates and patches that must be applied.  System Updates and Patches should be applied to applicable systems, following the policy and Standard Operating Procedures currently utilized for Lennar On-Premise systems.

### **Security Design/Architecture Overview** {#security-design/architecture-overview}

Coupled with the health check performed in Step 1, the intent of this process is to ensure that the general design and architecture of the environment follows the Lennar standard and/or adheres to commonly accepted practices defined by the industry.

This final Domain is focused on standardizing an approach and ensuring a consistent security architecture that can easily be integrated into the Enterprise Architecture as a whole.  Utilizing the Cloud Security Alliance v3.0 and the associated NIST 800-53R controls, common security patterns are adopted and used repeatedly.

1. The primary focus will be on the security boundaries (primarily internet-facing and On-Premise traffic), virtual network design and the use of Network Security Groups, and whether there is an adoption of ‘server-less’ solutions such as App Service, which tend to reduce the attack surface of cloud infrastructure.

2. In order to maintain standard configurations, Desired State Configurations and scripted extension deployments should be utilized to harden VM deployments and maintain consistent configurations. 

### **Risk Assessment and Auditing** {#risk-assessment-and-auditing}

1. Perform activities in STEP 1 again for validation (For existing systems and for assessment/analysis):  
1. As validation and to ensure that items are configured properly, the activities from STEP 1 will be executed and gathered again – Microsoft Azure Security Center Assessment, Advisor, and Monitor.  
2. Outstanding items should be remediated.  
3. The operationalization of the merged environments is a necessary final component.  The Lennar Security Operations Center will be updated with materials regarding new assets in the Lennar Production environment and alerts/logging that require their attention.  
4. Relevant Security Design Patterns/Architecture will be documented reflecting the end-state.

### **Application Service Environment** {#application-service-environment}

The **App Service Environment (ASE)** is a powerful feature in Azure that gives network isolation and improved scalability for our application development environment. An ASE is dedicated exclusively to a single subscription and can host 100 App Service Plan instances. 

|  ASE Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| ASE is a premium feature built into Azure’s application services our teams can use to isolate and improve our web apps, API apps, Mobile apps, and Azure functions. |
| ASE provides excellent performance, storage, memory, and fault tolerance capabilities while acting as a sophisticated solution to protect our most sensitive information. |

The range can span 100 instances in a single App Service plan to 100 single-instance App Service plans, and everything in between. It is essentially a deployment of dedicated computation resources which enable the resources to be used in an isolated manor.  App Service environments (ASEs) are appropriate for application workloads that require scalability, isolation, secure network access, and high memory utilization. ASEs are used to stand up single websites (web app service, sql database) and have an SQL backend for pushing website content. We isolate this push utility by disconnecting it from the web. Isolated websites are clustered in an ASE to interface with each other in a private subnet. This enables network control on PaaS resources. If the data categorization or application in an ASE is of platinum grade and deals with PII data, then it needs to be in an ASE to control.

### **Azure Policies** {#azure-policies}

Cloud policies in Azure ensure that Lennar is able to achieve its goals through an effective and efficient use of IT governance. It does this by creating clarity between business goals and IT projects through the use of automatic notifications and pushes based on our standards. 

|  Azure Policy Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Our automated tagging process saves us time and money over manually tagging each resource and ensures our IT standards are enforced in our subscriptions. |
| Azure is capable of leveraging policies that control and push automated scripts using our policy settings and implementing them at the resource group level for cost analysis report accuracy and budgeting. |

All of our services need tags associated with them in order to manage our Azure subscriptions. This policy is enforced using automated scrips that push and assign tags at the resource level. Essentially, instead of manual composing each tag, a script can run a policy that automatically tags resource groups with their appropriate meta-data every week. This removes the need to handle individual SNow requests for resource tagging and promotes ease-of-use engagement for our developers. Tagged resources can then be analyzed using our cost models in Cloudyn.

### **Azure Active Directory** {#azure-active-directory}

Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) is Microsoft’s multi-tenant, cloud-based directory and identity management service. Azure AD combines core directory services, advanced identity governance, and application access management.

|  Active Directory Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Azure AD offers a flexible way to set up authorization and authentication within applications that are built within Lennar’s cloud environment. |
| Azure AD centralizes identity and access management to enable deep security, productivity, and management across devices, data, apps, and infrastructure. |

Azure AD stores user name, passwords, profile data, and other information using tenants. A tenant is a logical construct that represents a secure, dedicated instance of Azure AD typically associated within Lennar’s data center infrastructure. When a security authentication request occurs within our domain, our ports connect it to our AD environment which automatically syncs it without Office365 service.

![][image22]

This model helps manage user identities and create intelligence-driven access policies to secure access for resource groups. Accessing Azure AD starts through Microsoft Azure Active Directory Connect (Azure AD Connect), a component of Azure AD that synchronizes user identity data between Lennar’s on-premises environment and Azure AD. Created resources are pushed and are then accessible by those with the required authorization.

### **Automatic Syncing** {#automatic-syncing}

Every 30-minutes, custom objects within our Lennar data center are pushed to our Active Directory environment using our access network. This syncs certain object categories within our Active Directory environment with our Azure Cloud environment so that our user accounts can be managed and updated. The Azure AD Connect sync manager displays logs from every delta sync including errors or warnings. These logs are stored on the AD server. Users access their accounts using OKTA as a single-sign on site to ensure our security authentication integrity is upheld when utilizing the strengths of the Azure Cloud environment.

The Azure AD Connect sync manager displays logs from every delta sync including errors or warnings. The logs are stored on the server. At Lennar, a local system account runs delta sync imports from the Lennar/Rialto AD forests to the Lennar Azure Active directory to force synchronization as applications are configured using Azure AD Connect ahead of the 30-minute sync.

## **Tagging**  {#tagging}

Tags provide a way to logically organize resources with properties that you define.  Tags can be applied to resource groups or resources directly. Tags can then be used to select resources or resource groups from the console, web portal, PowerShell, or the API. Tags are also particularly useful when you need to organize resource for billing or management.

|  Active Directory Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Tagging in Azure organizes our resource groups into manageable categories that make it easy to query and retrieve information, when requested, so that cost reports can be accurately run… (clarify). |
| Tags, which are strategically define so that a wide variety of cost reports can be setup and reported. We chose to tag and chose the tags we have is because we need to be able to run any variety of cost reports (project basis, application, environment) that can be requested. This is a flexible solution. |

You apply tags to your Azure resources to logically organize them by categories. Each tag consists of a name and a value. For example, you can apply the name "Environment" and the value "Production" to all the resources in production. After you apply tags, you can retrieve all the resources in your subscription with that tag name and value. Tags enable you to retrieve related resources from different resource groups. 

The following limitations apply to tags:

* Each resource or resource group can use 12 out of 15 tag name/value pairs. The remaining 3 are reserved for customized values for unique user needs.  
  * This limitation applies only to tags directly applied to the resource group or resource.   
  * A resource group can contain many resources that each have 15 tag name/value pairs.   
  * If you have more than 15 values that you need to associate with a resource, use a JSON string for the tag value.   
* The tag name is limited to 512 characters, and the tag value is limited to 256 characters. For storage accounts, the tag name is limited to 128 characters, and the tag value is limited to 256 characters.  
* Tags applied to the resource group are not inherited by the resources in that resource group.  
* Tag names can't contain these characters: \<, \>, %, &, \\, ?, /.

Every resource group **MUST** be tagged upon creation.  **Subsequently DSS will enforce a policy to tag every service that is created under that resource group.** This policy will run weekly to ensure every service is being tagged.  

### **Tag Structure** {#tag-structure}

The tag information below will be provided as part of the Service Now Cloud request ticket:

* Cost Center  
* Business unit or OU  
* Environment (PRD, QA, DEV, TEST, SBOX)  
* Business system / Application name  
* Application owner  
* Project name   
* SNow PRJ No.  
* RITM No.  
* Purchase Order No.  
* Data Classification (1,2,3)  
* Application classification (Platinum, Gold, Silver, Bronze)  
* Resource group name

Every time a tag is applied to a resource or a resource group, it overwrites the existing tags on that resource or resource group and must use JSON scripts to reassign the reformatted values using Azure’s PowerShell module.

## **Fault Tolerance** {#fault-tolerance}

Disaster recovery is an essential IT topic that concerns all data created and stored in Lennar’s datacenter. To ensure this data is protected from disaster, we have designed a high-availability architecture that ensures our application, database, VMs, etc., data is protected in the cloud. However, the means to which this protection is implemented remains to be decided. Regardless, there are two choices for cloud fault tolerance: replication or geo redundancy. 

|  Fault Tolerance Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Our fault tolerance process separates applications and services into a tiered hierarchy to enjoy the most important information is protected. |
| Azure allows us to use fault tolerance components for monitoring and alerts. |

By incorporating duplicate hardware configurations, one path of data or a server's physical hardware components can fail without affecting the operations of a server. An effective hardware strategy can improve the overall availability of a system. These strategies can range from adopting common sense practices to using expensive fault tolerant equipment.

Applications in Azure have different service level agreement metrics for uptime and storage. Lennar separates these into tiers: silver, gold, and platinum. While all applications are important, the costs associated with storage and disaster recovery demand a unique strategy to preserve our most important data over data that is deemed expendable.

![][image23]

Specifically, a fault tolerance solution at Lennar will depend on the costs associated with each application. This model will mature as more applications are created in our environment and evaluated used our cost management reports. 

## **Scripting**

Deploying Azure Resource Manager template files using the PowerShell module for Azure resource manager. Essentially, azure resource manager allows us to create ARM template, or a JSON template, which is an object notation file that contains a declarative syntax of cloud resources that we can deploy on Azure, such as SQL databases and web apps.

|  Azure Scripting Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| We chose ARM template scripting because it is the Microsoft accepted standard for automating the creation of services. |
| The amount of ARM templates already available in Azure for download works well with our continuous delivery and deployment goals for our future dev ops needs. |

Properly configured ARM template will allow our dev ops team to build applications and infrastructure services in the cloud. This starts with the Azure PowerShell module, which uses the ARM templates specifications to deploy services to our Azure subscriptions.

### **ARM Templates** {#arm-templates}

ARM templates can be found in GitHub and has a variety of services available for configuration. Any template created by Lennar’s dev ops team can be stored here and references for future use. It also acts as a repository by using imbedded settings in case our application services get deleted. New templates can be created on the Azure portal page in the Resource Manager module and existing templates can be found in Microsoft Azure’s GitHub page.

At this point we don’t have an official ARM repository that people can go to obtain Lennar approved templates. However, Lennar’s GitHub portal ([https://github.lennar.com](https://github.lennar.com)) will be the temporary repository for our saved templates. Microsoft’s public Azure Quick Start templates repository ([https://github.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates](https://github.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates)) is alspo available to use when designing Lennar templates. Once we have modified the templates from Microsoft to suit our cloud needs and standards, then we will store them somewhere in our Lennar GitHub.

![][image24]

Templates can contain one or more resource file, and after being submitted to the Azure Resource Manager API, the resources will be provisioned to users as directed. While on portal.azure.com, the automation scripts in a resource group can be reviewed and changed using ARM templates. The parameter options for the ARM template will appear in azure after downloading the template. Once configured, the Azure PowerShell module is used to deploy the services and provision them to users.

![][image25]

After an ARM template is set up and deployed using the PowerShell module, it’s provisioned in the appropriate subscriptions in Azure. Properly configured ARM templates can be copied and stored as application setting files. It’s important to keep these files with the template in GitHub to ensure they’re available in case their resources need to be restored.

## **Templates** {#templates}

Templates are used to provision subscription services to users in resource groups in Lennar’s Azure environment using the Resource Manager module. Before this functionality can be used, however, a ticket request must first be created in SNow and received by our administrators.

|  Resource Provisioning Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Templates help replicate our cloud tags and policies for new projects created by teams with the help of our administrators, free-of-charge. |
| SNow’s integration with Azure allows us follow Microsoft Azure’s best practices for subscription service provisioning when created and setting up service templates. |

The request and assignment process for subscription services requires a manager’s approval. If approved, tasks are created for the following teams:

* Systems Engineering Operations (SEO)  
* Network  
* Security

When tasks are completed and closed, their provisioned services are assigned to the Cloudyn team to create threshold report to track the increased cloud usage. When the services are provisioned properly, and the cost is accounted for, a task is assigned to security team for final review. Once complete, a notification is sent to requestor, approving manager, and Cloudyn team confirming the request was handled successfully. For production resources, a notification sent to SNow admin group to include in the configuration management database (CMDB).

### **Cloud Provisioning Templates** {#cloud-provisioning-templates}

Cloud Provisioning templates allow our system and application programmers to perform the following actions:

* Define the cloud domain, administrators for the domain, and classes of users (tenants) for the domain.  
* Prepare software services templates that provision subscription services.   
* Service providers can add templates, associate tenants with the templates, create resource pools for the templates, test the templates, then publish them to make them available for consumers.  
* Provision software from templates, creating software service instances.  
* Manage software services instances.

This meeting will discuss the details of the provisioning request, such as pricing for the cost center, duration, and monthly threshold values. Once this information is obtained, cloud service requests can be found in the SNow Cloud Services section of the Service Catalog tab.

![][image26]

Before any services can be provisioned, the requirements and documentation need to be defined in a discovery meeting with the following teams:

* Systems Engineering Operations (SEO)  
* Networks  
* Security  
* Database team  
* Web Team

#### ASP.Net Templates

Provisioning for PaaS resources requires specific templates to be used. ASP.NET templates are [open-source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_source) [server-side](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Server-side_scripting) [web application framework](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_application_framework)s designed for [web development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_development) to produce [dynamic web pages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dynamic_web_page).  These templates will be used to create Azure PaaS websites.

#### WordPress Templates

Azure has a greater advantage of implementing open source on the cloud and WordPress is a commonly used application to build various types of websites. Combining it with Azure is one of the best options.  These templates are used to provision Azure PaaS websites services to users.

## **Cloud Migration** {#cloud-migration}

The first governing factor in whether we migrate data from our on-premise environment to the cloud is cost. The second factor is efficiency. In this way, our migration model can be the most cost-effective and efficient method for managing our hybrid cloud solution. 

|  Cost Management Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Migrating our environment to the cloud using Azure ensures minimal downtime during the process as bulk service migration will be necessary to ensure all of our resources are available using secure access controls for our nation-wide team using an agile business model. |
| Azure cloud migration enables our data administrators to migrate services for hundreds of devices in a single day with only one team. |

The Migration of mission critical services such as email and CRM to Azure leverages requires our network to be optimize for the total server and storage space we’ll need for this to be successful. Using information from your discovery, map your servers to represent your on-premises applications. This will help you identify dependencies or communication between servers so you can include all necessary application components in your cloud migration plan—to help reduce risks and ensure a smooth migration. Group your servers logically to represent the applications, and then select the best migration strategy for each application based on its requirements and migration objectives.

![][image27]

When our application groups are mapped for migration, they must then be evaluated by how best to move each on-premises application to the cloud. This is done by calculating and comparing the total cost of ownership (TCO) for Azure with that of a comparable Azure deployment on our on-premise infrastructure.

# **Cost Management** {#cost-management}

Lennar manages our cloud spending using **Cloudyn**, a Microsoft application that monitors, allocates, and optimizes our cloud subscription costs. Cloudyn is accessed in Azure, where its dashboard shows sections on Lennar’s cloud costs, cost models, and account optimization information and can be configured to produce clear showback reports that extrapolate enterprise-wide subscription data on our cloud infrastructure usage.

|  Cost Management Standards Rationale |
| ----- |
| Cloudyn is included in the Azure platform, free-of-charge, and provides templates to report on costs and usage of our cloud service with a focus on eliminating idle resources. |
| Lennar’s Azure subscription service costs can be categorized using tags, date ranges, and cost models to accurately inform our teams of their cloud infrastructure budgeting. |
| Cloudyn is the adopted cost management solution built into the Azure portal and allows us to follow Microsoft’s best practices when it comes to cloud budgeting reports. |

Custom budget thresholds can be set up automatically to alert stakeholders when overspending is a risk as well as a custom report for projected costs when planning a project for approval. The cloud management dashboard breaks down our account costs and fractionates them into individual costs. 

Daily reports can be sent to the Cloudyn administration team on a custom schedule or when costs approach budget thresholds. These cost-over-time report displays trends in spending and can detect irregularities in deployment, such as loops or erroneous resource allocation.

Subscription accounts are reviewed in Azure’s Cost Management dashboard in the Cost Allocation 360 section. Clicking a panel will show you the reported information for the selected subscription accounts. 

![][image28]

Selecting tags in the side filters categorize the report to show the budget required to maintain these resources in the cloud over a specified date range. Once a report is configured, it must be saved as a draft and then saved again before it can be activated. It takes 24 hours before the cost model data can be updated in Cloudyn. This information can then be exported into an excel CSV under the **Actions** drop-down menu.

![][image29]

Threshold reports trigger when certain dollar amount limits are met for a project. These repots can trigger at 3 custom levels: 

* **Red** – Maximum cost threshold.  
* **Yellow** – Nearing max cost threshold.  
* **Green** – Normal cost status.

Reports are sent to the cloud administration team, the approving manager of the cloud, and the application owner so that each member of the project team is aware when costs meet their custom thresholds and may start charging for our data above the allocated budget amount.

![][image30]

### **Cost Model Allocation Reports** {#cost-model-allocation-reports}

When you start cost allocating our azure cloud budget, the first thing to define is the scope by using a cost model. Cost models segment cloud data by cost entity, account, or subscription, and can be ascribed multiple tags for resources and services leveraged by a team. These tags might be billing codes, a designated cost center, or a resource group name. Tags also help when performing showback reports for parts of Lennar’s organization.

To create a custom cost allocation model, select **Costs** \> **Cost Management** \> **Cost Allocation 360°** on the report's menu.

![][image31]

To view cost allocations by tags for a specific time frame for a cost analysis report:

1. In **Date Range**, select the date range for the time frame you want to analyze.

2. In **Tags**, select the tagged values you want to be included in the report and click **Add \+** to add additional tags.

3. In **Groups**, select the groups you want to be included in the report and click **Add \+** to add additional groups.

4. In **Filter**, use the dropdown menus to refine the fields in the results to specific items within each group, or select Show all to view all of the results for the group.

5. Use **Extended Filters** to define the criteria for the results displayed in the report:

* **Cost Model** - Select the Cost Model you would like to use. These are the policies you created in the Cost Allocation 360° Manager.  
* **Amortization** - Select whether you want to see the actual cost or the amortized cost.  
* **Allocation Rules** - If you want to see tagging without the Cloudyn cost allocation policies applied, (RDS, EC2, EBS) select Disable.  
* **Uncategorized** - costs can be shown or hidden.

8. In **Service**, select Show All to see all services that are running.

Groups of tagged resources thresholds can be shown in Cloudyn’s Optimizer, which is used to pull a list of accounts that aren’t utilizing their projected capacity and can be reduced for cost savings. Uncategorized resources explain rules for handling untagged.

![][image32]

To view the results of cost allocation, open the Cost Analysis report and select the cost model that you created. Then, add a grouping by one or more of the tags selected in the cost model. You can easily create and save reports that focus on specific services consumed by specific groups. This needs to be approved before any decisions are made.

### **Showback Report Process** {#showback-report-process}

All Cost Analysis reports support showback reports by displaying what resources they consumed. To view the results of cost allocation, open the Cost Analysis report and select a cost model. Then, add a grouping by one or more of the tags selected in the cost model.

![Cost Analysis report][image33]

You can easily create and save reports that focus on specific services consumed by specific groups. For example, you might have a department that uses Azure VMs extensively. You can create a report that's filtered on Azure VMs to show consumption and costs. This enables Our DSS team to run reports, such as one showing all the virtual machines that are not running in an optimized fashion.

### **Total Cost Overview Template** {#total-cost-overview-template}

To calculate our Azure account costs over a specified date range, follow the steps within Azure’s pricing calculator at [azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/calculator](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/calculator/?service=cost-management#cost-management4b242ecb-6621-4195-9080-1bf017b8a7cb). These costs can be analyzed for resources on cloud or on-premise in order to compare the cost between the two.

![][image34]

The enterprise architecture team can be contacted for the template our Azure costs. In the template, there is a table that outlines the requirements, see the **Configuration** column, can their costs from the calculator can be seen in the **Azure** column and **On Premise** column for comparison.

![][image35]

The exact costs are located in the **Parameters** sheet in the exported Excel TCO report. These parameters match the costs displayed in the calculator and are used in the formulas that calculate the cost-over-time budget amount for each environment.

![][image36]

The TCO report’s context is found in the **Assumptions** sheet in the TCO excel document. This provides the rationale behind the prices and needs that make up the subscription account’s services displayed in the cost-over-time model. Assumptions put down the pros and cons and base information that are not quantifiable. These are the basis and assumptions of some of the calculations in use.
